data.title: Hedge Funds Slam ‘Enormous Burden’ of New ESG Requirements (Bloomberg) -- Hedge funds are seizing the rising tide of opposition to European ESG rules as an opportunity to seek exemptions from some environmental, social and governance reporting requirements. Most Read from BloombergCitadel to Leave Namesake Chicago Tower as Employees RelocateState Farm Seeks Emergency California Rate Hike After FiresTransportation Memos Favor Places With Higher Birth and Marriage RatesSan Francisco Wants Wealthy Donors to Help Fix Fentanyl CrisisNY Transit Advocate Says
data.description:
(Bloomberg) -- Hedge funds are seizing the rising tide of opposition to European ESG rules as an opportunity to seek exemptions from some environmental, social and governance reporting requirements.

Most Read from Bloomberg

  • Citadel to Leave Namesake Chicago Tower as Employees Relocate

  • State Farm Seeks Emergency California Rate Hike After Fires

  • Transportation Memos Favor Places With Higher Birth and Marriage Rates

  • San Francisco Wants Wealthy Donors to Help Fix Fentanyl Crisis

  • NY Transit Advocate Says Billions in Tax Hikes Would Fix MTA

At issue is whether alternative investment managers should have to disclose ESG data on assets they invest in on behalf of their clients, under the European Union’s Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive. CSRD, which is designed to apply to all sectors, is currently the subject of intense debate as Germany and France seek to limit its scope. The EU’s financial services commissioner, Maria Luis Albuquerque, has said there’s room for adjustments in light of the criticisms.

It’s against that backdrop that the Alternative Investment Management Association, whose board includes Bridgewater Associates and Millennium Management, now says that its members should be exempt from reporting on clients’ assets.

“It’s creating an enormous burden on firms that really don’t have the sort of environmental or social footprint that a manufacturing company might,” said Adam Jacobs-Dean, global head of markets at AIMA, whose members oversee roughly $4 trillion of combined assets.

“And some of them won’t even have European investors or clients,” he said. “So who is the reporting for?”

The pushback from the hedge fund industry is the latest in a litany of complaints from business leaders and lawmakers who say ESG rulemaking in Europe has gone too far. They point to the bloc’s stagnating economy, and warn that competitiveness will slide further as President Donald Trump forces through a program of deregulation on the other side of the Atlantic.

Jacobs-Dean said hedge funds and private equity managers operating in the EU already provide the necessary data because they comply with the bloc’s ESG rulebook for the financial industry, the Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation.

Such efforts to resist ESG regulations have succeeded in the past. After heavy lobbying from banks, asset managers and insurers, the EU agreed last year to exclude those sectors from the full scope of the Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive, which exposes firms to litigation risk if ESG violations are found in their supply chains.

Similar concessions should now be applied to CSRD, Jacobs-Dean said. That would represent a “quick win,” he said.

The EU is due to propose changes to CSRD and several other pieces of legislation at the end of this month, as part of its so-called omnibus process. Officials have acknowledged the EU’s ESG framework needs to be adjusted but caution against expecting outright deregulation.

Among changes the EU is considering is matching the scope of CSRD to that of CSDDD, thereby reducing the number of companies affected by 85%, according to a report by Responsible Investor. Similar measures to shield smaller companies have been proposed by France and Germany.

Details of CSRD:

CSRD requires companies to disclose ESG data starting in 2024 annual reports.

MSCI Inc. says non-financial companies will, on average, disclose less than 500 data points on key themes like climate and labor conditions.

The commission’s omnibus bill is expected to look at simplifying CSRD, the Taxonomy Regulation and the Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive.

Beyond the hedge fund industry, asset managers more broadly are complaining that CSRD doesn’t make clear whether their reported value chains should include client assets. The question was to be clarified in guidance, but has been slowed as EU officials instead focus on adjusting ESG rules related to the needs of small and mid-sized companies.

“Due to this lack of certainty, there are diverging interpretations by the auditors on the scope of application,” said Laurence Caron-Habib, head of public affairs at BNP Paribas Asset Management.

Requests for clarification have now been sent to the commission from the Association of the Luxembourg Fund Industry, the Dutch Fund and Asset Management Association and the European Fund and Asset Management Association, whose members include Amundi SA, BlackRock Inc. and the investment arm of JPMorgan Chase & Co.

“We would like the forthcoming simplification omnibus to confirm our interpretation that CSRD client reporting is currently excluded until these issues are resolved,” said Andreas Stepnitzka, deputy director of regulatory policy at EFAMA, whose members manage $34 trillion.

A spokesperson for the European Commission said it’s looking into the matter.

Jenn-Hui Tan, chief sustainability officer at Fidelity International, said his firm is prepared to report both ways, “depending on how the regulation comes out.” But including client assets risks “potentially confusing messages,” when compared with SFDR reports.

Lawyers advising asset managers, meanwhile, are urging their clients to err on the side of caution. In the absence of clear guidance, “the prudent view is to assume that investments are in scope,” said Raza Naeem, a financial regulation partner at Linklaters.

Dan Grandage, chief sustainability officer at Abrdn Plc, said that for now, the goal is “to ensure that we complete the disclosures in a timely manner and well ahead of the deadline.”

DWS, the investment arm of Deutsche Bank AG, includes assets under management in CSRD reporting, said Piera Ziedek, team lead of integrated reporting and sustainability services at the firm.

It was “a judgment call that we made around our value chain,” Ziedek said.

As consumers of CSRD data, however, most asset managers are keen for the directive to move ahead.

For investors, it’s important to understand “whether a company aligns with the transition and if it stands to benefit from emerging opportunities or, conversely, faces transition risks,” said Elise Beaufils, deputy head of sustainability research at Lombard Odier Investment Managers.

Mandatory Reporting Standards under CSRD

--With assistance from John Ainger.

(Adds DWS comment in fourth-to-last paragraph.)

Most Read from Bloomberg Businessweek

  • Orange Juice Makers Are Desperate for a Comeback

  • Believing in Aliens Derailed This Internet Pioneer’s Career. Now He’s Facing Prison

  • Inside Elon Musk’s Attack on the US Government

  • Amazon and SpaceX Want In on India’s Satellite Internet Market

  • Elon Musk Inside the Treasury Department Payment System

data.title: Clean energy costs to continue to fall this year, report says The cost of clean energy technologies worldwide, such as wind, solar and battery storage, are expected to fall further this year, a report by BloombergNEF showed on Thursday, despite rising protectionism in the form of tariffs on green energy imports.  New wind and solar farms already undercut the production costs of new coal and gas plants in nearly every market worldwide.  Cheaper renewable energy enables countries to move away from fossil fuels in the fight against climate change.
data.description:
LONDON (Reuters) - The cost of clean energy technologies worldwide, such as wind, solar and battery storage, are expected to fall further this year, a report by BloombergNEF showed on Thursday, despite rising protectionism in the form of tariffs on green energy imports.

WHY IT'S IMPORTANT

New wind and solar farms already undercut the production costs of new coal and gas plants in nearly every market worldwide. Cheaper renewable energy enables countries to move away from fossil fuels in the fight against climate change.

CONTEXT

China’s clean technology manufacturing overcapacity drove cost declines last year and countries such as the U.S. and European nations have slapped tariffs on Chinese imports such as solar components and electric vehicles to protect domestic manufacturing.

On average, the China can produce a megawatt-hour of electricity from major power-generating technologies 11-64% cheaper than other markets, the report said.

BY THE NUMBERS

The cost of clean power technologies is expected to fall further by 2-11% in 2025. While trade barriers could stall declines temporarily, BNEF expects the levellized cost of electricity for clean technologies to fall by 22-49% by 2035.

KEY QUOTE

"China is exporting green energy tech so cheaply that the rest of the world is thinking about erecting barriers to protect their own industries," said Matthias Kimmel, head of energy economics at BNEF.

"But the overall trend in cost reductions is so strong that nobody, not even President Trump, will be able to halt it," he added.

(Reporting by Nina Chestney, Editing by William Maclean)

data.title: Frances Heaton, executive who became the first female head of the City Takeover Panel Frances Heaton, who has died aged 80, was director general of the City Takeover Panel and the first female member of the Court of the Bank of England.
data.description:
Frances Heaton, who has died aged 80, was director general of the City Takeover Panel and the first female member of the Court of the Bank of England.

The Panel on Takeovers and Mergers was established in 1968 under the Bank of England’s umbrella to act as the City’s referee in major deals involving public companies, with particular attention to fair treatment of shareholders. Its director general is traditionally a corporate finance practitioner seconded from one of the leading banks. Frances Heaton, a director of Lazard Brothers and former Treasury civil servant, held the post from 1992 to 1994 – the first woman following 10 men since the Panel’s creation.

After a rash of speculative takeovers in the 1980s boom and its aftermath, Frances Heaton defended the mechanism as an instrument for positive management change, but admitted: “Takeovers got a bad name because of the wave of financially oriented bids… I expect to see less of this in future.”

Several thorny issues crossed her desk in her first months in post, including a spat between HSBC and Lloyds Bank as potential bidders for Midland Bank. But one report called her overall tenure “untypically peaceful”, 1993 having been “one of the quietest years on record” in the takeover arena.

Her appointment to the Bank of England’s board of directors – known as the Court and chaired in that era by the Governor – was announced in February 1993. It attracted an annual fee of £500, fixed since the Bank was nationalised in 1946. Appointed alongside her were two City grandees, Sir Jeremy Morse and Sir Chips Keswick.

The Bank let it be known that she had been chosen for her track record in Whitehall as well as the Square Mile. The press hailed her as the first woman to breach the “all-male inner sanctum” of the Court since the Bank’s founding in 1694, following rumours that a female shortlist had also been considered for the governorship, for which Eddie George had been named as the next incumbent.

A high-principled professional and self-confessed workaholic, Frances Heaton did not enjoy dealing with the media, which tended to paint her as a rather aloof figure: one profile noted that “until recently she did not watch television.” When another journalist opened with: “Thank you for seeing me so quickly,” she replied: “Well I always believe in getting awful things over as soon as possible.”

She was also the only woman at that time to be named in a “Who’s Really Who” listing of the 100 most important people in the financial sector. But she herself played down what she called “this equality thing”, declaring firmly that “blend of experience” was a far more important factor in her rise. She was reappointed for a second four-year term on the Court from 1997 to 2001.

Frances Anne Whidborne was born in Winchester on August 11 1944 to John Whidborne, who farmed potatoes and hops in Hampshire and sat as a magistrate, and his wife Marjorie, née Maltby. Educated at Queen Anne’s School, Caversham, Frances went on to take a First in law at Trinity College Dublin and was called to the bar of Inner Temple in 1967.

Her civil service career began in the Wilson government’s Department of Economic Affairs. After its abolition in 1969 she spent a decade at HM Treasury working on assignments such as the establishment of the British National Oil Corporation and the ground-breaking sale of a first tranche of the government’s holding of BP shares in June 1977.

Later that year she was seconded to the merchant bank, SG Warburg. After a brief return to the Treasury, she opted for a permanent move to the City in 1980 – but the demands of working for Warburgs had been “terrible” for her family, she said later, and she found a better work-life balance (with “longer lunches”) at Lazard, where she was a director from 1987 to 2001.

In her later career Frances Heaton was a director of Commercial Union, Legal & General and Anglian Water, deputy chairman of the engineering group WS Atkins and chair of pension trustees for Lazard and Schroders. She was a member from 1997 to 2003 of the Committee on Standards in Public Life.

Beyond the City, Frances Heaton was a passionate equestrian and competitive event rider, who might have spent all day every day with her horses if life had been different. She was also a keen gardener at the farmstead in the South Downs which she and her husband refurbished.

She once observed that “I could have worked harder, ridden harder, seen more of my children. But there are only 24 hours in a day.” But her advice to others pursuing high-pressure careers was invariably: “don’t forget to have a family,” and she was devoted to her own.

Frances Heaton married, in 1969, Martin Heaton, a chartered surveyor whom she had met in their student days in Dublin. He survives her with their two sons.

Frances Heaton, born August 11 1944, died January 5 2025

data.title: Ford Stock Drops as Automaker Projects Lower Earnings, Offsetting Upbeat Q4 Results Ford Stock Drops as Automaker Projects Lower Earnings, Offsetting Upbeat Q4 Results
data.description:
KEY TAKEAWAYS

  • Ford shares are tumbling in Thursday morning after the automaker posted a downbeat outlook weighed on by losses in its electric vehicle business that offset higher-than-estimated quarterly results.

  • Ford Model e, the company’s EV division, reported a 2024 EBIT loss of $5.1 billion.

  • CEO Jim Farley said tariffs on Canada and Mexico would wipe out "billions of dollars of industry profits."



Ford (F) shares are tumbling Thursday morning after the automaker posted a downbeat outlook weighed on by losses in its electric vehicle (EV) business that offset higher-than-estimated quarterly results.

The Dearborn, Mich.-based automaker projected 2025 adjusted earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) of between $7.0 billion and $8.5 billion, marking a sharp decline from 2024’s $10.2 billion. The company attributed the soft outlook to "headwinds related to market factors."

Ford Model e, the company’s EV division, reported a 2024 EBIT loss of $5.1 billion, "as the company continues to invest in future products" and projected a 2025 EBIT loss of $5.0 billion to $5.5 billion. Chief Executive Officer Jim Farley said in an earnings call on Feb. 5—a transcript of which was made available by AlphaSense—that the EV market is facing "increased competition with increased pricing pressure."

Still, the company reported fourth-quarter revenue of $48.2 billion, up 5% year-over-year, and adjusted earnings per share (EPS) of 39 cents. Both surpassed consensus estimates from Visible Alpha.

Ford CEO Farley Notes Tariffs Would Hurt Industry

Farley said during the earnings call that the Trump administration's proposed tariffs on American trading partners would affect the industry as well as mean "higher prices for customers."

"There's no question that tariffs at 25% level from Canada and Mexico, if they're protracted, would have a huge impact on our industry with billions of dollars of industry profits wiped out and adverse effect on the US jobs, as well as the entire value system in our industry," Farley said.

Ford shares are down more than 6% in early morning trading Thursday. They have lost more than a fifth of their value in the past year.

Read the original article on Investopedia

data.title: Trump Treasury secretary Scott Bessent relives some pressure on the Fed Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Donald Trump is not asking the Federal Reserve to lower its short-term interest rates, but what he and the president do want is to bring down longer-term borrowing costs via 10-year Treasury yields.
data.description:
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Donald Trump is not asking the Federal Reserve to lower its short-term interest rates, but what he and the president do want is to bring down longer-term borrowing costs via 10-year Treasury yields.

"He and I are focused on the 10-year Treasury," Bessent said in an interview with Fox Business's Larry Kudlow, who was director of the National Economic Council during Trump's first term.

But Trump, he added, is "not calling for the Fed to lower rates."

While the Fed sets short-term borrowing rates that can have a domino effect and influence longer-term rates, there are other factors that buffet 10-year government bond yields — including the outlook for economic growth, inflation, supply of Treasuries and more.

The comments from Trump's new Treasury secretary marked the second time in a week that the White House has relieved some pressure on the Fed, which paused rates last month following three consecutive cuts as policymakers weigh the path of inflation and the effect of new economic policies from the Trump administration.

In fact, Trump himself said last Sunday that the central bank was right to keep interest rates unchanged at its last policy meeting on Jan. 29.

"I'm not surprised," he told reporters Sunday night. "I think holding the rates at this point was the right thing to do."

The president's comments about Fed monetary policy sounded like a reversal of sorts after Trump in late January said he would "demand" lower rates and that he thought the Fed would listen to him. He also said then that he expected to talk directly with Fed Chair Jerome Powell "at the right time."

Trump added in a Jan. 29 post on his social media platform that Powell and the central bank "failed to stop the problem they created" on inflation, without mentioning rates.

When Bessent discussed the Fed on Wednesday during his Fox interview, he made it clear he would only talk about what the Fed has already done — and not what he thinks the central bank should do from now on.

For example, he noted that when the Fed did a jumbo rate cut last September of 50 basis points, the yield on the 10-year Treasury went up.

Despite the Fed’s rate-cut campaign in late 2024, longer-term interest rates in the US increased sharply, which has meant higher rates on mortgages and other borrowings. That was due in part to investor expectations of higher inflation going forward.

Some economists are concerned that Trump's policies, including tariffs, could act as another source of upward pressure on prices.

Bessent said Wednesday that tariffs are a "means to an end," to bring manufacturing back to the US and re-shore economic security.

"In theory, tariffs would be a shrinking ice cube. That you would tariff a country, and then as the production comes back to the U.S., the income tax, the corporate revenues and the pay income tax goes up, and the tariff income would go down."

In turn, "the current account deficit goes down, our trade deficit goes down," he added.

The president, he noted, believes if the administration rolls back regulations, makes the 2017 Trump tax cuts permanent, and gets energy prices down then rates "will take care of themselves."

Bessent repeated his economic plan dubbed "3-3-3" — which aims for getting the deficit down to 3% of GDP from above 6% through cutting regulation, sustaining growth of 3% and boosting oil production by 3 million barrels a day.

And he offered some assurances about about Elon Musk and DOGE’s recent access to the Treasury Department’s critical payments systems.

"Our payment system is not being touched," said Bessent. "There's a study being done. Can we have more accountability, more accuracy, more traceability that the money is going where it is."

Click here for in-depth analysis of the latest stock market news and events moving stock prices

Read the latest financial and business news from Yahoo Finance

data.title: Britain is facing an economic crisis – Bailey must cut rates faster Jobs are being slashed, investment has been cancelled and retail sales are stagnant, while consumer and business confidence have been shattered. Talk to anyone in industry, and it is clear the UK is at risk of a deep recession.
data.description:
Jobs are being slashed, investment has been cancelled and retail sales are stagnant, while consumer and business confidence have been shattered. Talk to anyone in industry, and it is clear the UK is at risk of a deep recession.

The only people who haven’t yet noticed are the seven members of the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) who on Thursday voted for a token quarter-point reduction in interest rates.

The economy desperately needed a bold decision from the Bank – but it has failed yet again.

The data on the British economy keeps getting worse and worse. AstraZeneca has scrapped its planned vaccine plant. Lloyds has accelerated the closure of branches. Even garden centres, according to the Horticulture Trades Association this week, are cutting back on staff and stock.

When we get the GDP number for the latest quarter, it will surely have a minus sign in front of it. The only question is how much output will fall by.

You might think that all this means radical action is needed. A proactive central bank would be slashing rates, and perhaps even returning to quantitative easing, to stop output from completely collapsing. Instead, the MPC today delivered a modest quarter-point reduction.

Seriously? It is hard to figure out what the Bank is thinking. After all, it also halved its growth forecast, and warned that the outlook for the economy was darkening. If that does not justify a significant cut in rates then it is hard to know what does.

In fairness, some members of the MPC get what is happening.

Catherine Mann switched from being an uber-hawk to voting for a half-point reduction on Thursday (and, incidentally, made the case for her appointment as the first female governor if the hapless Andrew Bailey is forced out).

Swati Dhingra backed a half-point cut, too. But the rest of the committee decided to ignore what is happening in the real world.

There’s no doubt that Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, has dealt the MPC a terrible hand of cards. Her catastrophic decision to hammer companies with a huge rise in employment taxes, along with relentlessly talking down of the economy, is almost entirely to blame for the abrupt collapse of confidence.

At the same time, the Government’s extravagant public sector pay rises, and extra borrowing risk triggering a round of inflation. The MPC may feel it is not its job to fix a recession Reeves has created. Most of us will feel some sympathy if it wants to force her to face the consequences of her own arrogance and hubris.

But it doesn’t matter who’s fault this is. After all, a self-inflicted wound still hurts. And no one ever said central banking was easy.

The blunt truth is this: the UK is heading into a serious downturn, and if Donald Trump imposes tariffs, potentially a deep recession.

With the Office for Budget Responsibility breathing down her neck, Reeves may be hustled into an emergency Budget in the spring with yet more tax rises. A doom loop of rising taxes and falling output will be established that will be very hard to break.

The UK only had one escape route from that grim fate: a deep cut in rates. Unfortunately, the Bank flunked it.

data.title: Spotify, Warner Music Group sign new multi-year distribution deal Under the deal, Spotify will have a direct license from WMG's music publishing arm Warner Chappell Music, which has a catalog of over 1 million copyrights in the U.S. and several other countries.  The financial terms of the deal were not disclosed.  Spotify and WMG declined to comment when contacted by Reuters.
data.description:
(Reuters) - Spotify and Warner Music Group, which owns labels behind artists such as Ed Sheeran and Dua Lipa, signed a new multi-year deal for recording and publishing music, the companies said on Thursday.

Under the deal, Spotify will have a direct license from WMG's music publishing arm Warner Chappell Music, which has a catalog of over 1 million copyrights in the U.S. and several other countries.

The financial terms of the deal were not disclosed. Spotify and WMG declined to comment when contacted by Reuters.

The agreement between WMG and Spotity will expand paid subscription tiers, and introduce new content bundles, the companies said.

The Swedish streaming giant's deal with WMG follows a similar announcement with the world's biggest music label, Universal Music Group , which established a direct license between the two companies across Spotify's portfolio.

The new deals could help the streaming firm introduce new offerings to attract more subscribers. Spotify CEO Daniel Ek said on Tuesday that the company would have personalized offerings, including a premium tier called "superfans of music".

Separately, WMG, which also represents artists such as Adele, Wiz Khalifa and Madonna, acquired rights of a controlling stake in Tempo Music that it said would boost Warner Chappell's catalog.

WMG's shares were down 3.1% in premarket trading. Warner Music on Thursday also reported its first quarter results, with its $1.67 billion revenue coming in line with estimates compiled by LSEG.

(Reporting by Jaspreet Singh in Bengaluru, Editing by Leroy Leo)

data.title: Diabetes and obesity drugs fuel Eli Lilly profit in the final quarter of 2024 Eli Lilly’s profit doubled in the fourth quarter, propelled by its hot-selling diabetes and obesity treatments, and the drugmaker came out with a mostly better-than-expected 2025 forecast.  Sales of Lilly’s top-selling product, the diabetes treatment Mounjaro, jumped 60% to $3.53 billion in the final quarter of 2024 while its obesity treatment counterpart Zepbound brought in $1.9 billion.  Revenue from the breast cancer treatment Verzenio also helped, climbing 36% to $1.55 billion.
data.description:
Eli Lilly’s profit doubled in the fourth quarter, propelled by its hot-selling diabetes and obesity treatments, and the drugmaker came out with a mostly better-than-expected 2025 forecast.

Sales of Lilly’s top-selling product, the diabetes treatment Mounjaro, jumped 60% to $3.53 billion in the final quarter of 2024 while its obesity treatment counterpart Zepbound brought in $1.9 billion.

Revenue from the breast cancer treatment Verzenio also helped, climbing 36% to $1.55 billion.

Overall, Lilly’s quarterly profit swelled to $4.41 billion. Revenue advanced 45% to $13.53 billion, in line with expectations. Per-share earnings adjusted for one-time items totaled $5.32, easily topping the $5.01 that Wall Street was looking for, according to a poll of industry analysts by FactSet.

Mounjaro and Zepbound are part of a wave of diabetes and obesity medications known as GLP-1 receptor agonists that are soaring in popularity globally due to the amount of weight people lose while taking the injections. They compete with Ozempic and Wegovy from the Danish drugmaker Novo Nordisk, which also reported strong sales growth on Wednesday.

For 2024, Mounjaro sales more than doubled to $11.54 billion, while Zepbound notched sales of $4.9 billion in its first full year on the market.

Analysts expect more than $18 billion in sales from Mounjaro this year and over $10 billion from Zepbound, which was recently approved in the United States as a treatment for some forms of sleep apnea.

For 2025, the Indianapolis drugmaker expects adjusted earnings to range between $22.50 and $24 with revenue falling between $58 billion and $61 billion.

Analysts expect earnings of $22.77 per share on $58.8 billion in revenue.

Eli Lilly and Co. shares slipped 1% to $831.64 Thursday in premarket trading. The stock had already climbed 9% so far this year, as of Wednesday.

data.title: Revealed: how much poorer you are now than in 2020 A typical British family is as much as £15,000 per year poorer than they were five years ago, Telegraph Money analysis has found.
data.description:
A typical British family is as much as £15,000 per year poorer than they were five years ago, Telegraph Money analysis has found.

A heavier tax burden, higher mortgage rates and bigger energy bills have all strained household budgets since 2020.

While the Bank of England cut interest rates again on Thursday, the economic forecast is still bleak, with experts warning Chancellor Rachel Reeves may need to slash spending or cut taxes in an emergency Budget next month.

The pandemic and pent up housing demand

If few saw a global outbreak of Covid-19 coming, even fewer could have anticipated the consequences of lockdown. Consigned to the great indoors for leisure and work alike, demand for floorspace boomed.

Detached properties fetched roughly £350,000 on average in January 2020, according to HM Land Registry. By the time all restrictions had ended, in the latter half of 2021, this figure was £50,000 higher.

And this was only the beginning – annual price growth hit a record of almost 15pc in mid-2022.

On the eve of 2025 – the latest data relating to last November – a family home with a garden was more likely to be worth £440,000. That’s a gain of almost 25pc.

If the family weren’t fortunate enough to be cash buyers, the mortgage market deals a further blow here.

Bank of England data shows the average five-year fixed-term rate offered to those putting up a 25pc deposit was 1.7pc at the turn of 2020. 4.4pc would be the deal on paper now.

For the same property bought in 2020, such less favourable rates pushed repayments up by some £300 a month. Over the course of a year, that’s nearly £3,800 extra to find between the sofa cushions.

Lagging wages and the affordability squeeze

Our analysis focused on price rises for a typical family made up of working parents with two children.

If each parent made the median employee salary for their gender in 2020, the Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings (ASHE) would see Dad gross just over £31,000 and Mum (more likely to be in a part-time role) just under £21,000.

That financial year, during which Rishi Sunak led the Treasury, the couple would have netted just over £43,000 after income tax and National Insurance contributions.

Had the couple received pay rises in line with national averages, they would have a £12,000 uplift in their take home pay over the five years.

The latest available salaries data relates to 2024, but assuming the Office for Budget Responsibility’s nominal earnings growth projections hold (and Labour refrains from an emergency tax-raising Budget in the next few months), their combined take-home pay rises to £55,000.

Stealth taxes alone – the result of said Chancellor freezing the thresholds beyond which workers cross into higher tax bands – will have cost the family almost £3,000 in total during this time.

Overall, however, largely thanks to Jeremy Hunt’s National Insurance breaks, while pre-tax income to the family increased by some 28pc, their tax bill grew by a lesser 24.5pc.

Stagnating standards of living

But this is scarcely reflective of how well-off the household is likely to feel. Using house prices as a yardstick, their lot has essentially stagnated.

In 2020, the detached home they were after would have cost roughly 8.3 years-worth of their joined after-tax earnings. After creeping beyond nine years in 2022, this ratio sat closer to eight in 2025.

With the cost of living as a benchmark, the story is far bleaker. In real terms – adjusting for the spiralling inflation of the past few years – Mum and Dad’s disposable income increased by just 3.4pc.

Energy crisis

A number of big ticket items have a hand in this, perhaps none more viscerally so for British consumers than energy bills.

Emergence from the pandemic brought with it strong upward pressure on oil, gas and electricity prices. The reduction in supply from Russia’s invasion of Ukraine only made matters worse.

Wholesale prices hit record highs during the ensuing energy crisis, and business as usual is yet to resume, according to the National Grid – the spot price was just over 4 pence per kWh on the January 1, over four times the going rate at the start of 2020.

Designed to spare domestic users from sky-high bills, regulator Ofgem’s energy price cap shot up 54pc in April 2022.

The current cap, what a typical dual fuel household can expect to pay in a year, is £1,738 – over £700 more than what it was at the same point in 2020.

And then there’s council tax. From £1,750 back in the day, the average Band D rate in England, including all eventual precepts and mayoral fees, is up to £2,171 – with further rises widely expected come April.

The day to day

Little if anything has been spared from this trend. The Office for National Statistics (ONS) regularly surveys households to see how much they’re spending on all of life’s needs, from bread to coffee to painkillers and mobile internet bundles.

Over the tax year ending in 2020, excluding housing costs, these amounted to some £19,000. In 2025, the same level of consumption is £5,000 dearer at £24,000.

This includes a £1,100 increase from food and drink, an extra £250 on clothes and shoes, as well as a £850 addition to eating out and staying in hotels.

The call to upgrade transport means on top would not be one to be taken lightly. The Volvo XC60 emerged as Auto Trader’s “Best Car for Families” back in 2020. The automatic petrol variant cost £41,860 to put on the road at the time, the brochure shows.

An upgrade to the Swedish carmaker’s 2025 edition would set our family back £47,615.

And so, with a far-from-exhaustive, illustrative tour of expenses, an increase in costs to the tune of £15,000 emerges. Balanced against likely salary boosts, £3,000 in savings are needed to make ends meet like they used to.

Broaden your horizons with award-winning British journalism. Try The Telegraph free for 1 month with unlimited access to our award-winning website, exclusive app, money-saving offers and more.

data.title: Pop-Tarts maker Kellanova beats quarterly sales, profit estimates on steady demand While other packaged food companies such as Conagra and McCormick are facing a demand slowdown as customers cut back on ready-to-eat meals, Kellanova has managed to drive demand by diversifying its offerings.  The Pop-Tarts maker is also seeing customers choose its cereals and frozen breakfast as they pare back spending at restaurants to save money.  As a result, Kellanova said it would not provide a full year forecast.
data.description:
(Reuters) - Packaged food company Kellanova beat Wall Street expectations for fourth-quarter earnings on Thursday, on the back of steady demand for its snacks and breakfast cereals such as Pringles and Cheez-It.

While other packaged food companies such as Conagra and McCormick are facing a demand slowdown as customers cut back on ready-to-eat meals, Kellanova has managed to drive demand by diversifying its offerings.

Kellanova has introduced products such as Tresor Brownie and also partnered with Taco Bell to add Cheez-It to the fast food chain's menus.

The Pop-Tarts maker is also seeing customers choose its cereals and frozen breakfast as they pare back spending at restaurants to save money.

The company is in the process of being bought out by family-owned snacking company Mars. As a result, Kellanova said it would not provide a full year forecast.

Kellanova has also seen a jump in its gross profit margin through the year, mainly driven by higher prices and easing input cost inflation, helping offset increased marketing spend.

Volume grew 2.8% in the quarter, even as pricing rose 4.2%.

The company posted net sales of $3.12 billion, beating estimates of 3.10 billion according to data compiled by LSEG.

The company earned 92 cents per share in the quarter ended Dec. 28, beating expectations of 83 cents.

(Reporting by Aamir Sohail in Bengaluru; Editing by Krishna Chandra Eluri)
